data.title: THE INSTRUCTION BUDGET, AND WHY TWO FILES BEAT ONE
blocks: Here is something most people don't know when they first start working with Claude Code: every session begins with a hid
The instruction budget, and why two file — Claude Code loads its own system prompt at the start of every session — Anthropic's instructions about how to behave as

data.title: THE FOUR-MOVE PROMPT.
blocks: Move 1: Show what you have.
Move 2: Say what you want.
Move 3: Constrain it.
Move 4: Ask for verification.
Always specify the D3 version.

data.title: THREE FAILURE MODES.
blocks: API hallucination — Code calls d3.scale.linear() (v3 syntax) or invents methods that don't exist
Chart-type mismatch — Pie chart for 14 categories, line chart for unordered groups
Channel mismatch — Hue encoding a quantitative variable, area encoding when length would work

data.title: MULTI-LLM COMPARISON
blocks: targeted
Chart selection.
Accessibility decisions.

data.title: THE VERIFICATION STACK.
blocks: Layer 1: Sanity-check the format.
Layer 2: Check specific facts.
Layer 3: Test the work.

data.title: A WORKED EXAMPLE.
blocks: · Identify each data attribute and classify as categorical, ordered,
· Identify the most important attribute given the communication goal.
· Recommend a chart type by applying Munzner's expressiveness and
· Specify the marks and channel-to-attribute mappings precisely enough
· Flag any channel used redundantly. Justify the redundancy.

data.title: EXERCISES
blocks: · You want to ask whether a Sankey diagram is appropriate for a dataset you describe in words.
· You are building 15 charts across a semester-long project, each in its own HTML file.
· You need Claude to read a published chart on a webpage and critique its encoding decisions.
· You want to generate a single bar chart from data you can describe in three lines.
